text: From the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. You want this to happen any more? Sure. Today is Monday, July 25, 2011. My name is Joe Manier of the Southern World History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapelville. I'm with our project, Video of the Fert John Fisher in Ithaca, New York, to do an oral history interview for the Civil Rights History Project, which is a joint undertaking of Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress. And we're delighted this morning to be with you, Ms. Cotton, and Ms. Dorothy Cotton. Thank you, I'm here to hear your Ithaca to spend some time talking about Civil Rights History. Thank you. Let me ask a very broad first question for some reflections I have read where you wrote that you wrote the follow and kind of thinking about the world that you came up in. You wrote from a very early age I felt like I was in the wrong place. Yes. It's not interesting. It is to me that the sense that I was in the wrong place was very, just sort of pervasive in my being. I couldn't put words on it at the time that I was feeling that as a youngster. But of course, as the years have moved on and I've moved on, I still reflect on it and have learned to have come clear on just that fact that I was supposed to be somewhere else. This is not my home community even. It was alien, alien to something in my very being. I don't know if you know something about that neighborhood. 917 North Greenlee Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, where I was born. And outhouse in the backyard and people fighting and doing horrible things to each other. And that must have had something to do with it. Just the behavior of the people, not just the behavior. I don't know the way they were, the people in that neighborhood felt alien to me even as a child. And I think you lost your mother. My mother died when I was three years old. And of course, that was the depression and segregated southern town. What can you say to kind of frame the circumstances of your father's life after having lost his wife and the poor children's arrays? What do I say about my father now? And in these days, incidentally, my feeling about my father, my understanding of my father has evolved. When we were children, he was just the big guy. And he really wasn't big at all, skinny guy actually. But here's this big man raising full girls. And I know a lot, and I understand a great deal about him in retrospect. I know now that he went to, I think he said the third grade. And that's what stands out for me that he went to the third grade. Now, no matter did he just get to the third grade, they lived way out somewhere. I must research that place on somebody's farm, kind of a plantation kind of place. And his mother worked for a white family. It's interesting. I sort of choke on that kind of language now. I really don't even, one day, if I get, if I'm on the planet longer, if I'm going to create a whole new genre of language with which to describe people, because I'm really, I've jumped forward here. But it seems so just silly, ridiculous to me to continue to refer to people based on a color. As I said to a friend recently, well, she wasn't even a friend. I was doing a speech and I pointed to a woman sitting down front with a white blouse on. I said, they call you a white woman. But you know what, if you were the color of your blouse, I would call the ambulance and get you to a hospital. And my dress that I was wearing was black. My skin, Andrew Jung calls it, I was a pecan tan. And so there's nothing old here. You know, it's not literal. But anyway, I sort of digress here. But I think about my father with a great deal of sadness now. And but also some anger, I can forgive to some degree the things that I, well, the part of his behavior that makes me angry and that is still hurtful, because I'm wise enough now to accept the fact that he didn't know any better. For example, the way he would discipline us, these four, I'm three. We were one two, less than one year old, three and four, the four of us, when my mother died. His notion of how you teach children and punish them was to take a strap and they were not soft blows. I can still feel anger about that. But I have very mixed feelings about him because again, that's what was done to him. And I learned that very late in life that his mother would come home from taking care back to the color thing again. This white family and their children, his mother would come home late at night having left her children all alone. When she'd get home and my dad's older sister, Aunt Penny said this to me. I said, I'm Penny, how are you all punished? Now, after I'm now reflecting on the ridiculousness and the pain of the way he punish does. And Penny, his sister said, well, when Mama would get home, she would find a reason to whip all of us with a switch. Sometimes they had to go get the switch. And it's even more horrible things than that. So I have such mixed feelings. Fast forward to the mixture now as I look back at my dad and as I do things that would be just beyond his imagination. I was in South Africa and I thought, oh, my dad could have had the experience of traveling to South Africa. If my dad, I mean, I've been in Chai, I've been in a lot of countries. If he could, my dad never even had an airplane ride. And even going to an airport was very special for him. Because that was not a part of his world. Even when we were all out of the house and one of my mother's sisters passed by and my dad was sitting on the porch, and you're by himself and my aunt said, Claude, get in the car and come go with me up to Buffalo, New York. My dad, sitting on the porch and Goesborough, North Carolina, jumped in my car. Had no plans to do that. Got up to Buffalo, New York, and set up his house keeping function there. So my, the sadness that I feel now is I'll give you a little anecdote and then I'll stop talking about him. We went, I flew there and to see him at some point. And after he'd been there a few years. And my dad, I said, can you want to go to the airport with us on a cousin who was living there. Drove us to the airport. And it was only five or six of us. And my dad, he was working at the Bethlehem Steel mills there. And when we finished eating in the airport, my dad pulled out his wallet and paid something like $42 for the five or six of us to eat. You could imagine that was some years ago. You wouldn't, you wouldn't eat for five or six people for that amount of money. One dinner would cost that now. And, and I remember much later, but that was very special for him. First of all, to be in the airport. And, and to pay that because my dad would have paid, would have had less than that amount of money for a week's work when we were children in Goldberg, North Carolina. Working in this tobacco factory. Back to Goldberg now in the tobacco factory. His job was to put tops on barrels where he'd have to use a hammer. And I'm not sure what it's called. It's a hammer on one side and an axe. Because he chopped a part of his finger off with that axe one day. Axe, hammer, I'm not, again, not sure what it's called. But, so he had that they did let him come home from work. And he got him to the hospital first and they sort of sold it back on what they did. But you could tell, he'd been injured. But back to the, now having made his way to Buffalo, New York. Being in the airport was very special. Another time when I had flown there, a cousin who lives there took me to the airport to get my ticket. And I was there to see him. When I got back home, he was sullen and quiet back to his house. And he was hurt because he didn't get to go to the airport. That was so special for him. Before I cry, I'm going to stop talking about him. It's a weird, it's just, it's very painful. The deprivation and what was so special to him. But, well, one way to speak of my dad was a real mixture. My own feeling is first of all, a real mixture of feelings. First, I can still be angry, feel angry. I feel the anger about the beatings for the, you break a dish. You know, he's got his belt off. But I can also remember, at Christmas time, he had a smile on his face when he could get off over a doll. And a T-set and our own box of fruit. And he was a shoe box. And he was so proud that he was able to do that. So, you can see, it was such a, oh, there must be a word for this. Such a mixture, both in terms of my own feelings and my own assessment of him. And his own feelings about his life and his work. But right now, in the year 2011, most of my feelings, sort of about my dad, revolves around the deprivation of his life, his treatment. My dad was really nervous in front of white folks. And so, that's, it's a real mixture of my feelings about him. A kind of love for him. An understanding of the sort of mixed, that must be word for this as well, feeling about his life and his relationship to us. It's positive, just a second. We'll go back on after a short break. Ms. Cotton, let me ask you about, I think in your life, as sometimes happens, as folks come up, there was a teacher, a high school teacher in your place. Oh, yes. There was, it would be a very tremendous influence. And it helped you take a look step forward towards college and probably. I thought you were going to ask me what made me, there's a little story I want to tell you about, I think I was around 10 years old, back on Greenleaf Street at North Goalsboro. When a little white boy was riding his bicycle down my block, kicking up dust, because the pavement stopped at that, it got through the white section. And he was singing, you may be familiar with the song, deep in the heart of Texas, where he was singing, deep in the heart of Nigger Town. I wanted to tell you that because I have never, ever forgotten that that made me angry. That little white boy was riding down my street singing that. Now, want to go back to high school? Okay, that's what my high school days. Yes, I wish I could thank her in person, but Ms. Rosa Gray was my English teacher. Ms. Rosa Gray was not only the English teacher, she was responsible for the annual play for the high school. And I got the leading role in the high school play every single year. And I felt such a connection to her. But even before probably more than being aware and conscious of how special it was, that she would name me this poor little girl from Greenleaf Street. But the teachers were poor too, compared to today. We had to, in the English classes, one day we had been required to memorize some poetry. And I don't remember what the poem was, but I do remember that as I stood before the class and recited the poem, and as I headed back to my seat, she said very audibly, there's your ready girl till this day that phrase, that description just goes around and around in my consciousness. There's your ready girl. But I knew that, I think I'm going to cry. I knew that I never wanted to disappoint her. I wish there was something, not too much, that is the personal thing necessarily, but was she something of a surrogate mother since you had lost your mother? It's interesting that you say that. I never thought of her as a surrogate mother, but maybe she was a surrogate mother, because I had no mother. She would... This is... Did you take it on that? Yeah, let's just take a little break. Okay, we're back after a short break. We're talking about Miss Gray. Of course, my sister would tease me a little bit, and when I talk about my fondness for her and my members of her, excuse me, relationship with my English teacher, she's... Are you sure you didn't love Miss Gray? She was going to make sure you got lunch. But she was... We all had a little laugh about that, because I was... I wasn't feeling... Anyway, I had... Just some other feelings... My sister made me laugh. But another example... It's the notion of Miss Gray being a surrogate mother is what really got to me, because I never thought of it that way, but I think that's true. She... There's the junior senior prom in high school, and when it was time to go to the junior senior prom, my dad was not going to let me go. Miss Gray came to my house, sat on the porch, and those little houses along there, those little three little row houses, and they're all torn down now. But the porch was high, so I wanted to get under the porch. My dad was sitting on the front porch, and my teacher, Miss Rose Gray, sat there, offered her a chair, and she was pleading with him to let me go to the junior senior prom. She said, she would... Shaperone me? I don't know if dad knew what the word Shaperone meant, bless his soul. But I think he said she would take care of me. But she was pleading with him to let me go. I mean, I'm her star pupil, and I can't go to the junior senior prom. And I remember that I, under the house, under the porch listening, that I was really upset, and fast forward, believe it or not, 30 or 40 years later. It came clear to me why my dad said I could not go. I'm sure my dad, as poor as he was, was a very proud man. He would not let me go to the junior senior prom, and that's what could be as dressed up as any other child. He had no money for that. He couldn't tell her that. He said, he said, I just, he wasn't ready for my girls to go to dances. That's what he said. He was going to talk proper for the teacher. I told you he went to the third grade and living out on that farm. Did you come up in the church? Not really. My dad had a brief marriage to someone who was, I guess they married yet. It's interesting that I never knew, but she was there as the stepmother, Mrs. and me. And I don't know what her name was before. I'm pretty sure they married, but I don't know anything about the ceremony or anything like that. I don't know how many years, but it didn't last long. And fast forward to a time when we all grown and married and living our various lives, my three sisters and I. And my dad, confessing to us that I wasn't loving Susie Mae. Those are his exact words. I just brought her in there to help take care of you, young ones. So that's why we had this stepmother for a while. And the stepmother back to church was very much a church-going woman. I remember once she was taking all four of us to church and there was a very threatening storm was pending, thunder and lightning. And we were walking to the church and all four of us in tow with Mrs. and me. And we always called them Mrs. and me. And when she decided she needed to go back to the house because the lightning was so strong and fierce. And we turned around and went back to the house. And as she got to the house, she said, that won't, nothing but the devil. And so she was very much into church. So she would put this on the devil and to keep her from going to church. So that was a memory I have of Mrs. Mae. I don't know when she left or how she left, but it wasn't long. Fast forward, we reconnected with her like 40 or 50 years later. And she died. Now, but we reconnected with her. But she married. And she married someone she called in Bishop somebody and was living in Florida. And I saw her. Yeah, Mrs. Mae. She, yeah. Okay, I got to short break. Yeah. I want to tell you, my dad, I never knew my dad to sit foot in the church except when his mother died. As a matter of fact, I think my dad was anti church because I remember his saying, we were, I guess, late teenagers. Then somebody who was calling himself a preacher said, my dad said, that ain't nothing but his racket. Maybe true folks, some of the folks who call themselves Reverend as well. He's a way to make sure you have it income was my dad's thought. Nothing but a racket. But when he, I remember his going, of course, when his mother died, and we were all there, of course. And I remember his crying in the church bending over with his handkerchief to his face. But other than that, my dad, but my mother, my actual blood mother, my, I knew her father. He was a preacher. And, and I, a tenorant preacher went from a small town to small town. And I think he and my dad actually hated each other. And my dad did confess, I'm not sure in what context, but he used the word stole. In other words, they looked, Maggie, my mother's name. She was quite young. I'm not sure how old, but quite young when my dad had married her, but they ran off and got married. And I think that grandpa, her father, Mr. Reverend Seymour Pellum, and Reverend Seymour Pellum would come by the house. We did call him grandpa, but he would, I could just tell that the energy wasn't right in the house when grandpa stopped by. Let me take you back to Miss Grandford just a moment. Oh, yes. As I understand, she would be, she was the part of the story. She's in the story regarding your transition to Rolly to a ten shop. Yes. Miss Gray, but we used to sing a song. They used to sing people, church-going people in those days used to sing a song. I know the Lord laid his hands on me, meaning that as a metaphor, you know, that she laid her hands on me in a very special way, in other words. Sort of took control of just really special events, or control of me in a special way. But Miss Gray knew that college was not in the offing for me, but she called over to Shaw University when I was about to graduate from high school. And talked to, I don't know, who, how, one got to go, who you talked to to get to go to college. But Miss Gray talked to someone there at Shaw, and I ended up being accepted as a student at Shaw University. She had gotten two jobs for me. One job was working in the dining hall. The other job was cleaning the teacher's dormitory on the campus at Shaw University. Eventually, well, eventually it makes it sound like a long time, but it wasn't very long before I was offered a job to be, they didn't use the term, but a housekeeper in the president's residence. For a very short time, I had three jobs working my way through college. My dad never gave me a dime. I don't blame him for that. He didn't have any money to, and probably didn't know what college was, not really. But so I was cleaning the teacher's dormitory, and eventually I was able to give up one of the jobs. I think that was the one that I gave up, and still worked in the dining hall for a little while. But when I must have been a good worker and a good cleaner in the teacher's dormitory, because I was invited to come and be a housekeeper for the president. Dr. Robert Printis Daniel, and his wife Blanche Daniel, they had me coming over to instead of the teacher's dormitory to help take care of the president's residence. Dr. and Mrs. Daniel had no children, and I had a sense that ultimately, in a way I was part daughter and part housekeeper. I worked very hard in that house, though, and it was a three-story house. I stayed through graduation and getting married. I felt like I was part daughter and part housekeeper. The housekeeping part, or even being with them, the Daniels, the president of Shaw University. It was not a happy relationship, so I'm taking care of the house. Sometimes I get a feeling that they wanted me to act like a daughter. That too was a very complicated relationship, because they weren't really nice to me. I remember once I had the flu, and Dr. Daniel said, you can't just go to bed because you don't feel well. He even said that to me. I got a check for a fast forward job in the library. That's really quite forehead, but I'm still living there. Mrs. Daniel opened the envelope. She wanted to see how much I was baking. I have to tell you a little anecdote about my dad. My dad came from Goldsboro while I was a student there at Shaw, and I'm now in the president's home. My dad had never seen the flowers in the president's residence. He said, what in the world does anybody want that flower shining like this for? I was the one who polished those flowers and put the wax on the flowers or whatever for a while. Then they had another fellow Robinson. This all I remember is Robinson. It's never knew his first name would come and do some things in the house. But I became sort of a, I think in a way they loved having me there, having no children of their own. But at the same time, I don't have happy memories of being there, but it was helpful because they saw that. I went to class. It took me at least six years to get my college degree. I was in a class with a fellow, I think his name is Harris Sims, was a classmate of mine. In a class, Shakespearean class, we both loved Shakespeare. I remember that poem. I said in her class, I love reciting Shakespearean poetry. And this fellow, Harris Sims sat next to me in this Shakespearean class studying Shakespeare. And we used to walk down the hill, Harris Sims and I, to have a beverage. We didn't have a campus center as they do on campuses now. But there was a fellow who had a place for students to hang out and have refreshments just down the hill from Virginia State College. And sitting there one day of Sims said to me, I have a fellow I'd like you to meet. And his name is George Cotton, fast forward, you can imagine. So George Cotton, this, quite, must have been, George was at least ten years older than I, but that was not an issue then. And I remember that they had this great big Buick. And I had the sense that, anyway, George Cotton took a liken to me. And I've read somewhere that people say I met George Cotton in college suggesting that he was a student as well. But he was not. George never went to college. I don't know how far George Cotton went to school, but even. But Cotton got to be quite heavy fast forward to a time when I actually said, actually I didn't say yes. He didn't ask me to marry him. He showed up one day with a ring and very correctly wanted to speak to Dr. Daniel. And I knew at that point what he was going to do. I didn't know he had a ring, but he wanted to ask for my hand. I wonder what George learned that. I stood at the top of the stairs holding onto the banister. And I heard George saying to Dr. Daniel that he wanted to marry me and he wanted their blessing. And I remember that Dr. Daniel said to George, D'Artha is very ambitious. You sure you want this marriage. And George said, yes sir. George Cotton and I got married in the president's residence, this place where I scrubbed for the worst. Not scrubbed polished floors, then cooked, served meals. And again, I was a housekeeper and part daughter I used to say. But a very mixed, I could say a lot about the mixture of my energy. One of those questions on that point and maybe this will illustrate that attention. When Dr. Daniel made the transition from Shaw to Virginia State in 1950, and you moved and shifted your college to the moment up there. Or you? Yeah, I'm still at Shaw. And what I was just saying was that Virginia State College at that point. When I moved, when Dr. Daniel was offered the president's of Virginia State College, I guess I was a really good house cleaner because they wanted me to go with them. Yeah, I was ahead of the story because all of it, what I was just talking about happened at Virginia State College. I was at Shaw probably no more than one year, one school year. And went to Virginia State College because Dr. Daniel became president of Virginia State College. And this is where, and this is where I mentioned the classmate, Sims, and I meant, anyway, I'm sorry. No, no, I was just curious. Yeah, but that was interesting because I was thinking about being on the campus, but I need to, which campus am I talking about now? When Dr. Daniel got the offer? Of course, it would be in Petersburg where you would be drawn after college more directly into early movement activities. Well, I got to Virginia State College. And that Virginia State College, again, that's where this Sims, and I were in the Shakespearean class, that was not as short, was at Virginia State College. And George Cotton lived there in Pita's bird. And when I said yes, I would marry him and even had the ring, I think he gave me the ring that later on that evening. And there was a little wedding right there in the house. I meant. That's a policy for Christus. Do you not have a turn to... Okay, we're back down. That's a little break. For the telephone. So did we get married here? We were very... We were talking about how you would be drawn into... I want to tell you about a little boy, because I consider this the beginning. Not so much drawn into the movement, but a particular consciousness was evolving. And back to my childhood, when... Did I tell you about the little boy driving down the sidewalks, seeing deep in the heart of Nigger Town? Okay, I know that a consciousness about the wrongness of this system was that opened it up for me. Okay, fast-forward to Virginia State College. And when I became active in a church in... Pita's bird, Virginia. I got to sit his right here. And the Reverend Wyatt T. Walker was pastor of this church. And I'm not sure why I started going there, except that Wyatt T. Walker was also the regional head of the NAACP. And also the other... I think it seems like all of the organizations really related were very close to Congress or racial equality and at the time. And I think I was drawn to that church because of his... He was also a really good preacher. And he also was a very handsome guy. We used to tease each other because my house became the house after getting really involved with all of his extracurricular activities and also very active in the church. Even on programs sometimes still doing poetry. I remember reading poetry from the balcony in the church. And it was just really fun hanging out with Reverend Walker. So being active, he's running the NAACP and becoming concerned that Black folk could not use the public library. He took a real initiative there around that library discrimination in the library. And I thought, just imagine we can't use the public library and yet they, white folks, call us ignorant. And I felt that very strongly now as... I'm not sure exactly what year it was, but that will be easy to find. I may remember it in just a moment here. But Reverend Walker asked me to... He saw my interest if I would help the... In this organizing and training of children, especially younger people, because he wanted us to put a picket line in front of the library and ultimately in front of the Woolworth store downtown. But we started with the library and I found a letter as a matter of fact because I became secretary to him, secretary of the NAACP. And I had to therefore just really kind of be with him regularly in that work and handling the communication. So I was trained, ended up training youngsters in the church to walk with picket signs and how... And I had heard something of non-violence and we invited this young preacher from Montgomery, Alabama, Martin Luther King to come up to Petersburg, Virginia. To speak for work because we now started having what we call mass meetings as well in Petersburg. Reverend Walker had met Martin at a conference at Virginia Union University in Richmond, Virginia. I remember he was telling me that. He had met him and now Reverend Walker is very much interested in what was going on in Montgomery, Alabama, the Montgomery Bus Boycott. And invited this little preacher from Montgomery to come up and speak for us. Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King did just that because in cities where there was a protest activity and it seemed to be just springing up all over the place, this protest activity. And so now everybody had a mass meeting. We call them mass meetings. This is for some reason. Well, I think maybe it's obvious. The mass meeting interested the meetings and all those cities and definitely in Petersburg, whatever where. People were interested in coming to the mass meetings because they wanted to hear what was going on in town. So they started coming to the mass meetings and when everybody now, massive numbers of people were hearing about the Montgomery Bus Boycott and Rosa Parks. And when he came up to speak for us, Dr. King, I was on the program. You know, when Black folks have programs with God, music and dancing and poetry and singing and everybody's involved, I used to try to tell the students at Cornell University when we pay all this money for a speaker to come. And they just have the speaker walk up and very coldly give his speech and walk off with $10,000. Put some flowers on the table. Let's have some music. Let's do something. So maybe that's a cultural difference, I don't know. But I did poetry back to my love of poetry again. I remember that we had dinner at the Parcinage at Reverend Walker's house. And the young women's parish club was the organized group of women in the church that was hosting and planned the dinner and we were serving the dinner. And I was moving around the table from the kitchen to the table. And Dr. King wanted to know, I can see him now sitting at the end of this quite long table in the Parcinage at Reverend Walker's house. And he wanted to meet that young lady who said the poem on the program earlier that night. Somehow the meal I think was after the program. And so I was identified. It was Dorothy Cotton who said the poem on the program. And he wanted to meet me and I stood by the head of the table where he was sitting or maybe it was the foot of the table, one end of the table. And I told him about the point. And we had a nice little chat. He was a user to me, very unassuming little guy, you know, rather short of statue. As Correra used to say, she was not impressed when she saw him in terms of statue or anything like that. But there was still something very impressive about him. And I enjoyed standing there chatting with him at the table because we were the young women's Paris Club. We were serving the meal. While he was there in Petersburg, he was very much interested in what we would, our protest activity, picketing in front of the library. And I had run the workshops, knew nothing substantive about non-violence. But I knew that you were not supposed to fight or hit anybody back. Very simplistic at that time. But I'm helping the youngsters prepare to walk in front of the dime store, Woolworth store, and in front of the library. But apparently before Dr. King left Petersburg, he asked Revan Walker to move to Atlanta. And while she walked, Revan Walker said, I will move to Atlanta to work with you as you are developing this organization, the Southern Christian Leadership Conference. I'll move there and work with you. If I can take the two people who helped me here, that was a fellow named Jim Wood and me. If he could take us. And so, while I Walker raised the subject with me and would I be interested in going, and I said to my husband, George Cotton, I told him this door. And I said, I'd like to go down and help them out for about three months. And George was very quiet, didn't say very much. I'm not sure what he felt. I can summarize. Now, many, many, many years later, the pain it must have been for him because I went. George Cotton, my husband, drove me to Atlanta. And Revan Walker was in the process of moving and I got to Atlanta and to stay my three months that I promised my husband. And George, as I said, drove me there. And would come down once or twice, he came down in the early months. But I stayed 23 years. That the movement became my life once I, but that's how I got there. He's coming up to visit Dr. King, coming to visit us in Petersburg. And then my going going with to work with Revan Walker. And ultimately becoming, I ended up being a director of education for SCLC. Exactly. We'll turn to that. Just once I, one quick little question about the timing in Petersburg. It's not crucial. We could look this up easily enough. But you're, I believe the protest activity that you and in Petersburg and other folks carried forward out of the church in the Pittsburgh Improvement Association. That, that began in 59, I think. Do you remember? I think the Greensboro City in February, 1960. Oh, I'd have to look at the dates. It would be at the moment. That's okay. But I think you guys were even, you know, picketing in front of the Greensboro City. And then of course, after that, things really exploded. I was not picketing in Greensboro. No, no, but you mean the movement, some movement people. Oh, yeah. Oh, no, your, your pickets in front of the library and in front of wallboards. Yeah, okay. Okay. Were those before? Oh, I see what you, I'll see what you're saying. I'd have to, I'd have to research that. It's not a crucial thing. Yeah, I'd have to look at it. But interesting question that you raised. Now I'm curious about it. Tell me about, tell me about what you found when you arrived in Atlanta to join this organization called the Southern Fisheries Conference, which had only a very, very small step. segments.text: From the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
You want this to happen any more?
Sure.
Today is Monday, July 25, 2011.
My name is Joe Manier of the Southern World History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapelville.
I'm with our project, Video of the Fert John Fisher in Ithaca, New York, to do an oral history interview for the Civil Rights History Project,
which is a joint undertaking of Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress.
And we're delighted this morning to be with you, Ms. Cotton, and Ms. Dorothy Cotton.
Thank you, I'm here to hear your Ithaca to spend some time talking about Civil Rights History.
Thank you.
Let me ask a very broad first question for some reflections I have read where you wrote that you wrote the follow and kind of thinking about the world that you came up in.
You wrote from a very early age I felt like I was in the wrong place.
Yes.
It's not interesting.
It is to me that the sense that I was in the wrong place was very, just sort of pervasive in my being.
I couldn't put words on it at the time that I was feeling that as a youngster.
But of course, as the years have moved on and I've moved on, I still reflect on it and have learned to have come clear on just that fact that I was supposed to be somewhere else.
This is not my home community even.
It was alien, alien to something in my very being.
I don't know if you know something about that neighborhood.
917 North Greenlee Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, where I was born.
And outhouse in the backyard and people fighting and doing horrible things to each other.
And that must have had something to do with it.
Just the behavior of the people, not just the behavior.
I don't know the way they were, the people in that neighborhood felt alien to me even as a child.
And I think you lost your mother.
My mother died when I was three years old.
And of course, that was the depression and segregated southern town.
What can you say to kind of frame the circumstances of your father's life after having lost his wife and the poor children's arrays?
What do I say about my father now?
And in these days, incidentally, my feeling about my father, my understanding of my father has evolved.
When we were children, he was just the big guy.
And he really wasn't big at all, skinny guy actually.
But here's this big man raising full girls.
And I know a lot, and I understand a great deal about him in retrospect.
I know now that he went to, I think he said the third grade.
And that's what stands out for me that he went to the third grade.
Now, no matter did he just get to the third grade, they lived way out somewhere.
I must research that place on somebody's farm, kind of a plantation kind of place.
And his mother worked for a white family.
It's interesting. I sort of choke on that kind of language now.
I really don't even, one day, if I get, if I'm on the planet longer,
if I'm going to create a whole new genre of language with which to describe people,
because I'm really, I've jumped forward here.
But it seems so just silly, ridiculous to me to continue to refer to people based on a color.
As I said to a friend recently, well, she wasn't even a friend.
I was doing a speech and I pointed to a woman sitting down front with a white blouse on.
I said, they call you a white woman.
But you know what, if you were the color of your blouse, I would call the ambulance and get you to a hospital.
And my dress that I was wearing was black.
My skin, Andrew Jung calls it, I was a pecan tan.
And so there's nothing old here.
You know, it's not literal.
But anyway, I sort of digress here.
But I think about my father with a great deal of sadness now.
And but also some anger, I can forgive to some degree the things that I,
well, the part of his behavior that makes me angry and that is still hurtful,
because I'm wise enough now to accept the fact that he didn't know any better.
For example, the way he would discipline us, these four, I'm three.
We were one two, less than one year old, three and four, the four of us, when my mother died.
His notion of how you teach children and punish them was to take a strap and they were not soft blows.
I can still feel anger about that.
But I have very mixed feelings about him because again, that's what was done to him.
And I learned that very late in life that his mother would come home from taking care back to the color thing again.
This white family and their children, his mother would come home late at night having left her children all alone.
When she'd get home and my dad's older sister, Aunt Penny said this to me.
I said, I'm Penny, how are you all punished?
Now, after I'm now reflecting on the ridiculousness and the pain of the way he punish does.
And Penny, his sister said, well, when Mama would get home, she would find a reason to whip all of us with a switch.
Sometimes they had to go get the switch.
And it's even more horrible things than that.
So I have such mixed feelings.
Fast forward to the mixture now as I look back at my dad and as I do things that would be just beyond his imagination.
I was in South Africa and I thought, oh, my dad could have had the experience of traveling to South Africa.
If my dad, I mean, I've been in Chai, I've been in a lot of countries.
If he could, my dad never even had an airplane ride.
And even going to an airport was very special for him.
Because that was not a part of his world.
Even when we were all out of the house and one of my mother's sisters passed by and my dad was sitting on the porch,
and you're by himself and my aunt said, Claude, get in the car and come go with me up to Buffalo, New York.
My dad, sitting on the porch and Goesborough, North Carolina, jumped in my car.
Had no plans to do that.
Got up to Buffalo, New York, and set up his house keeping function there.
So my, the sadness that I feel now is I'll give you a little anecdote and then I'll stop talking about him.
We went, I flew there and to see him at some point.
And after he'd been there a few years.
And my dad, I said, can you want to go to the airport with us on a cousin who was living there.
Drove us to the airport.
And it was only five or six of us.
And my dad, he was working at the Bethlehem Steel mills there.
And when we finished eating in the airport, my dad pulled out his wallet and paid something like $42 for the five or six of us to eat.
You could imagine that was some years ago.
You wouldn't, you wouldn't eat for five or six people for that amount of money.
One dinner would cost that now.
And, and I remember much later, but that was very special for him.
First of all, to be in the airport.
And, and to pay that because my dad would have paid, would have had less than that amount of money for a week's work when we were children in Goldberg, North Carolina.
Working in this tobacco factory.
Back to Goldberg now in the tobacco factory.
His job was to put tops on barrels where he'd have to use a hammer.
And I'm not sure what it's called.
It's a hammer on one side and an axe.
Because he chopped a part of his finger off with that axe one day.
Axe, hammer, I'm not, again, not sure what it's called.
But, so he had that they did let him come home from work.
And he got him to the hospital first and they sort of sold it back on what they did.
But you could tell, he'd been injured.
But back to the, now having made his way to Buffalo, New York.
Being in the airport was very special.
Another time when I had flown there, a cousin who lives there took me to the airport to get my ticket.
And I was there to see him.
When I got back home, he was sullen and quiet back to his house.
And he was hurt because he didn't get to go to the airport.
That was so special for him.
Before I cry, I'm going to stop talking about him.
It's a weird, it's just, it's very painful.
The deprivation and what was so special to him.
But, well, one way to speak of my dad was a real mixture.
My own feeling is first of all, a real mixture of feelings.
First, I can still be angry, feel angry.
I feel the anger about the beatings for the, you break a dish.
You know, he's got his belt off.
But I can also remember, at Christmas time, he had a smile on his face when he could get off over a doll.
And a T-set and our own box of fruit.
And he was a shoe box.
And he was so proud that he was able to do that.
So, you can see, it was such a, oh, there must be a word for this.
Such a mixture, both in terms of my own feelings and my own assessment of him.
And his own feelings about his life and his work.
But right now, in the year 2011, most of my feelings, sort of about my dad,
revolves around the deprivation of his life, his treatment.
My dad was really nervous in front of white folks.
And so, that's, it's a real mixture of my feelings about him.
A kind of love for him.
An understanding of the sort of mixed, that must be word for this as well,
feeling about his life and his relationship to us.
It's positive, just a second.
We'll go back on after a short break.
Ms. Cotton, let me ask you about, I think in your life, as sometimes happens,
as folks come up, there was a teacher, a high school teacher in your place.
Oh, yes.
There was, it would be a very tremendous influence.
And it helped you take a look step forward towards college and probably.
I thought you were going to ask me what made me, there's a little story I want to tell you about,
I think I was around 10 years old, back on Greenleaf Street at North Goalsboro.
When a little white boy was riding his bicycle down my block, kicking up dust,
because the pavement stopped at that, it got through the white section.
And he was singing, you may be familiar with the song, deep in the heart of Texas,
where he was singing, deep in the heart of Nigger Town.
I wanted to tell you that because I have never, ever forgotten that that made me angry.
That little white boy was riding down my street singing that.
Now, want to go back to high school?
Okay, that's what my high school days.
Yes, I wish I could thank her in person, but Ms. Rosa Gray was my English teacher.
Ms. Rosa Gray was not only the English teacher, she was responsible for the annual play for the high school.
And I got the leading role in the high school play every single year.
And I felt such a connection to her.
But even before probably more than being aware and conscious of how special it was,
that she would name me this poor little girl from Greenleaf Street.
But the teachers were poor too, compared to today.
We had to, in the English classes, one day we had been required to memorize some poetry.
And I don't remember what the poem was, but I do remember that as I stood before the class
and recited the poem, and as I headed back to my seat, she said very audibly,
there's your ready girl till this day that phrase, that description just goes around and around in my consciousness.
There's your ready girl.
But I knew that, I think I'm going to cry.
I knew that I never wanted to disappoint her.
I wish there was something, not too much, that is the personal thing necessarily,
but was she something of a surrogate mother since you had lost your mother?
It's interesting that you say that.
I never thought of her as a surrogate mother, but maybe she was a surrogate mother,
because I had no mother.
She would...
This is...
Did you take it on that?
Yeah, let's just take a little break.
Okay, we're back after a short break.
We're talking about Miss Gray.
Of course, my sister would tease me a little bit, and when I talk about my fondness for her and my members of her,
excuse me, relationship with my English teacher, she's...
Are you sure you didn't love Miss Gray?
She was going to make sure you got lunch.
But she was...
We all had a little laugh about that, because I was...
I wasn't feeling...
Anyway, I had...
Just some other feelings...
My sister made me laugh.
But another example...
It's the notion of Miss Gray being a surrogate mother is what really got to me,
because I never thought of it that way, but I think that's true.
She...
There's the junior senior prom in high school, and when it was time to go to the junior senior prom,
my dad was not going to let me go.
Miss Gray came to my house, sat on the porch, and those little houses along there,
those little three little row houses, and they're all torn down now.
But the porch was high, so I wanted to get under the porch.
My dad was sitting on the front porch, and my teacher, Miss Rose Gray,
sat there, offered her a chair, and she was pleading with him to let me go to the junior senior prom.
She said, she would...
Shaperone me?
I don't know if dad knew what the word Shaperone meant, bless his soul.
But I think he said she would take care of me.
But she was pleading with him to let me go.
I mean, I'm her star pupil, and I can't go to the junior senior prom.
And I remember that I, under the house, under the porch listening,
that I was really upset, and fast forward, believe it or not, 30 or 40 years later.
It came clear to me why my dad said I could not go.
I'm sure my dad, as poor as he was, was a very proud man.
He would not let me go to the junior senior prom, and that's what could be as dressed up as any other child.
He had no money for that.
He couldn't tell her that.
He said, he said, I just, he wasn't ready for my girls to go to dances.
That's what he said.
He was going to talk proper for the teacher.
I told you he went to the third grade and living out on that farm.
Did you come up in the church?
Not really.
My dad had a brief marriage to someone who was, I guess they married yet.
It's interesting that I never knew, but she was there as the stepmother, Mrs. and me.
And I don't know what her name was before.
I'm pretty sure they married, but I don't know anything about the ceremony or anything like that.
I don't know how many years, but it didn't last long.
And fast forward to a time when we all grown and married and living our various lives, my three sisters and I.
And my dad, confessing to us that I wasn't loving Susie Mae.
Those are his exact words.
I just brought her in there to help take care of you, young ones.
So that's why we had this stepmother for a while.
And the stepmother back to church was very much a church-going woman.
I remember once she was taking all four of us to church and there was a very threatening storm was pending, thunder and lightning.
And we were walking to the church and all four of us in tow with Mrs. and me.
And we always called them Mrs. and me.
And when she decided she needed to go back to the house because the lightning was so strong and fierce.
And we turned around and went back to the house.
And as she got to the house, she said, that won't, nothing but the devil.
And so she was very much into church.
So she would put this on the devil and to keep her from going to church.
So that was a memory I have of Mrs. Mae.
I don't know when she left or how she left, but it wasn't long.
Fast forward, we reconnected with her like 40 or 50 years later.
And she died.
Now, but we reconnected with her.
But she married.
And she married someone she called in Bishop somebody and was living in Florida.
And I saw her.
Yeah, Mrs. Mae.
She, yeah.
Okay, I got to short break.
Yeah.
I want to tell you, my dad, I never knew my dad to sit foot in the church except when his mother died.
As a matter of fact, I think my dad was anti church because I remember his saying,
we were, I guess, late teenagers.
Then somebody who was calling himself a preacher said, my dad said,
that ain't nothing but his racket.
Maybe true folks, some of the folks who call themselves Reverend as well.
He's a way to make sure you have it income was my dad's thought.
Nothing but a racket.
But when he, I remember his going, of course, when his mother died,
and we were all there, of course.
And I remember his crying in the church bending over with his handkerchief to his face.
But other than that, my dad, but my mother, my actual blood mother,
my, I knew her father.
He was a preacher.
And, and I, a tenorant preacher went from a small town to small town.
And I think he and my dad actually hated each other.
And my dad did confess, I'm not sure in what context, but he used the word
stole.
In other words, they looked, Maggie, my mother's name.
She was quite young.
I'm not sure how old, but quite young when my dad had married her,
but they ran off and got married.
And I think that grandpa, her father, Mr. Reverend Seymour Pellum,
and Reverend Seymour Pellum would come by the house.
We did call him grandpa, but he would, I could just tell that the energy wasn't right
in the house when grandpa stopped by.
Let me take you back to Miss Grandford just a moment.
Oh, yes.
As I understand, she would be, she was the part of the story.
She's in the story regarding your transition to Rolly to a ten shop.
Yes.
Miss Gray, but we used to sing a song.
They used to sing people, church-going people in those days used to sing a song.
I know the Lord laid his hands on me, meaning that as a metaphor, you know,
that she laid her hands on me in a very special way, in other words.
Sort of took control of just really special events, or control of me in a special way.
But Miss Gray knew that college was not in the offing for me,
but she called over to Shaw University when I was about to graduate from high school.
And talked to, I don't know, who, how, one got to go, who you talked to to get to go to college.
But Miss Gray talked to someone there at Shaw, and I ended up being accepted as a student at Shaw University.
She had gotten two jobs for me.
One job was working in the dining hall.
The other job was cleaning the teacher's dormitory on the campus at Shaw University.
Eventually, well, eventually it makes it sound like a long time, but it wasn't very long before I was offered a job to be,
they didn't use the term, but a housekeeper in the president's residence.
For a very short time, I had three jobs working my way through college.
My dad never gave me a dime.
I don't blame him for that. He didn't have any money to, and probably didn't know what college was, not really.
But so I was cleaning the teacher's dormitory, and eventually I was able to give up one of the jobs.
I think that was the one that I gave up, and still worked in the dining hall for a little while.
But when I must have been a good worker and a good cleaner in the teacher's dormitory,
because I was invited to come and be a housekeeper for the president.
Dr. Robert Printis Daniel, and his wife Blanche Daniel, they had me coming over to instead of the teacher's dormitory to help take care of the president's residence.
Dr. and Mrs. Daniel had no children, and I had a sense that ultimately,
in a way I was part daughter and part housekeeper.
I worked very hard in that house, though, and it was a three-story house.
I stayed through graduation and getting married.
I felt like I was part daughter and part housekeeper.
The housekeeping part, or even being with them, the Daniels, the president of Shaw University.
It was not a happy relationship, so I'm taking care of the house.
Sometimes I get a feeling that they wanted me to act like a daughter.
That too was a very complicated relationship, because they weren't really nice to me.
I remember once I had the flu, and Dr. Daniel said, you can't just go to bed because you don't feel well.
He even said that to me.
I got a check for a fast forward job in the library.
That's really quite forehead, but I'm still living there.
Mrs. Daniel opened the envelope. She wanted to see how much I was baking.
I have to tell you a little anecdote about my dad.
My dad came from Goldsboro while I was a student there at Shaw, and I'm now in the president's home.
My dad had never seen the flowers in the president's residence.
He said, what in the world does anybody want that flower shining like this for?
I was the one who polished those flowers and put the wax on the flowers or whatever for a while.
Then they had another fellow Robinson.
This all I remember is Robinson.
It's never knew his first name would come and do some things in the house.
But I became sort of a, I think in a way they loved having me there, having no children of their own.
But at the same time, I don't have happy memories of being there, but it was helpful because they saw that.
I went to class. It took me at least six years to get my college degree.
I was in a class with a fellow, I think his name is Harris Sims, was a classmate of mine.
In a class, Shakespearean class, we both loved Shakespeare.
I remember that poem. I said in her class, I love reciting Shakespearean poetry.
And this fellow, Harris Sims sat next to me in this Shakespearean class studying Shakespeare.
And we used to walk down the hill, Harris Sims and I, to have a beverage.
We didn't have a campus center as they do on campuses now.
But there was a fellow who had a place for students to hang out and have refreshments just down the hill from Virginia State College.
And sitting there one day of Sims said to me, I have a fellow I'd like you to meet.
And his name is George Cotton, fast forward, you can imagine.
So George Cotton, this, quite, must have been, George was at least ten years older than I, but that was not an issue then.
And I remember that they had this great big Buick.
And I had the sense that, anyway, George Cotton took a liken to me.
And I've read somewhere that people say I met George Cotton in college suggesting that he was a student as well.
But he was not. George never went to college. I don't know how far George Cotton went to school, but even.
But Cotton got to be quite heavy fast forward to a time when I actually said, actually I didn't say yes.
He didn't ask me to marry him. He showed up one day with a ring and very correctly wanted to speak to Dr. Daniel.
And I knew at that point what he was going to do. I didn't know he had a ring, but he wanted to ask for my hand.
I wonder what George learned that.
I stood at the top of the stairs holding onto the banister.
And I heard George saying to Dr. Daniel that he wanted to marry me and he wanted their blessing.
And I remember that Dr. Daniel said to George, D'Artha is very ambitious.
You sure you want this marriage.
And George said, yes sir.
George Cotton and I got married in the president's residence, this place where I scrubbed for the worst.
Not scrubbed polished floors, then cooked, served meals.
And again, I was a housekeeper and part daughter I used to say.
But a very mixed, I could say a lot about the mixture of my energy.
One of those questions on that point and maybe this will illustrate that attention.
When Dr. Daniel made the transition from Shaw to Virginia State in 1950, and you moved and shifted your college to the moment up there.
Or you?
Yeah, I'm still at Shaw.
And what I was just saying was that Virginia State College at that point.
When I moved, when Dr. Daniel was offered the president's of Virginia State College,
I guess I was a really good house cleaner because they wanted me to go with them.
Yeah, I was ahead of the story because all of it, what I was just talking about happened at Virginia State College.
I was at Shaw probably no more than one year, one school year.
And went to Virginia State College because Dr. Daniel became president of Virginia State College.
And this is where, and this is where I mentioned the classmate, Sims, and I meant, anyway, I'm sorry.
No, no, I was just curious.
Yeah, but that was interesting because I was thinking about being on the campus, but I need to, which campus am I talking about now?
When Dr. Daniel got the offer?
Of course, it would be in Petersburg where you would be drawn after college more directly into early movement activities.
Well, I got to Virginia State College.
And that Virginia State College, again, that's where this Sims, and I were in the Shakespearean class, that was not as short, was at Virginia State College.
And George Cotton lived there in Pita's bird.
And when I said yes, I would marry him and even had the ring, I think he gave me the ring that later on that evening.
And there was a little wedding right there in the house.
I meant.
That's a policy for Christus.
Do you not have a turn to...
Okay, we're back down.
That's a little break.
For the telephone.
So did we get married here?
We were very...
We were talking about how you would be drawn into...
I want to tell you about a little boy, because I consider this the beginning.
Not so much drawn into the movement, but a particular consciousness was evolving.
And back to my childhood, when...
Did I tell you about the little boy driving down the sidewalks, seeing deep in the heart of Nigger Town?
Okay, I know that a consciousness about the wrongness of this system was that opened it up for me.
Okay, fast-forward to Virginia State College.
And when I became active in a church in...
Pita's bird, Virginia.
I got to sit his right here.
And the Reverend Wyatt T. Walker was pastor of this church.
And I'm not sure why I started going there, except that Wyatt T. Walker was also the regional head of the NAACP.
And also the other...
I think it seems like all of the organizations really related were very close to Congress or racial equality and at the time.
And I think I was drawn to that church because of his...
He was also a really good preacher.
And he also was a very handsome guy.
We used to tease each other because my house became the house after getting really involved with all of his extracurricular activities and also very active in the church.
Even on programs sometimes still doing poetry.
I remember reading poetry from the balcony in the church.
And it was just really fun hanging out with Reverend Walker.
So being active, he's running the NAACP and becoming concerned that Black folk could not use the public library.
He took a real initiative there around that library discrimination in the library.
And I thought, just imagine we can't use the public library and yet they, white folks, call us ignorant.
And I felt that very strongly now as...
I'm not sure exactly what year it was, but that will be easy to find.
I may remember it in just a moment here.
But Reverend Walker asked me to...
He saw my interest if I would help the...
In this organizing and training of children, especially younger people, because he wanted us to put a picket line in front of the library and ultimately in front of the Woolworth store downtown.
But we started with the library and I found a letter as a matter of fact because I became secretary to him, secretary of the NAACP.
And I had to therefore just really kind of be with him regularly in that work and handling the communication.
So I was trained, ended up training youngsters in the church to walk with picket signs and how...
And I had heard something of non-violence and we invited this young preacher from Montgomery, Alabama, Martin Luther King to come up to Petersburg, Virginia.
To speak for work because we now started having what we call mass meetings as well in Petersburg.
Reverend Walker had met Martin at a conference at Virginia Union University in Richmond, Virginia.
I remember he was telling me that. He had met him and now Reverend Walker is very much interested in what was going on in Montgomery, Alabama, the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
And invited this little preacher from Montgomery to come up and speak for us.
Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King did just that because in cities where there was a protest activity and it seemed to be just springing up all over the place, this protest activity.
And so now everybody had a mass meeting. We call them mass meetings. This is for some reason. Well, I think maybe it's obvious.
The mass meeting interested the meetings and all those cities and definitely in Petersburg, whatever where.
People were interested in coming to the mass meetings because they wanted to hear what was going on in town.
So they started coming to the mass meetings and when everybody now, massive numbers of people were hearing about the Montgomery Bus Boycott and Rosa Parks.
And when he came up to speak for us, Dr. King, I was on the program.

You know, when Black folks have programs with God, music and dancing and poetry and singing and everybody's involved, I used to try to tell the students at Cornell University when we pay all this money for a speaker to come.
And they just have the speaker walk up and very coldly give his speech and walk off with $10,000.
Put some flowers on the table. Let's have some music. Let's do something.
So maybe that's a cultural difference, I don't know. But I did poetry back to my love of poetry again.
I remember that we had dinner at the Parcinage at Reverend Walker's house.
And the young women's parish club was the organized group of women in the church that was hosting and planned the dinner and we were serving the dinner.

And I was moving around the table from the kitchen to the table. And Dr. King wanted to know, I can see him now sitting at the end of this quite long table in the Parcinage at Reverend Walker's house.
And he wanted to meet that young lady who said the poem on the program earlier that night.
Somehow the meal I think was after the program. And so I was identified. It was Dorothy Cotton who said the poem on the program.
And he wanted to meet me and I stood by the head of the table where he was sitting or maybe it was the foot of the table, one end of the table.
And I told him about the point. And we had a nice little chat. He was a user to me, very unassuming little guy, you know, rather short of statue.
As Correra used to say, she was not impressed when she saw him in terms of statue or anything like that. But there was still something very impressive about him.
And I enjoyed standing there chatting with him at the table because we were the young women's Paris Club. We were serving the meal.
While he was there in Petersburg, he was very much interested in what we would, our protest activity, picketing in front of the library.
And I had run the workshops, knew nothing substantive about non-violence. But I knew that you were not supposed to fight or hit anybody back. Very simplistic at that time.
But I'm helping the youngsters prepare to walk in front of the dime store, Woolworth store, and in front of the library.

But apparently before Dr. King left Petersburg, he asked Revan Walker to move to Atlanta. And while she walked, Revan Walker said, I will move to Atlanta to work with you as you are developing this organization, the Southern Christian Leadership Conference.
I'll move there and work with you. If I can take the two people who helped me here, that was a fellow named Jim Wood and me. If he could take us.
